perturbation.original_text: The Partnership, the Partners and Manager desire to amend the Agreement to extend the term of the Agreement and to add a new section concerning curtailment of the LIFO Partners (as defined below), all as set forth in this Amendment. NOW, THEREFORE, IN CONSIDERATION of the foregoing premises, the parties hereby agree as follows: 1. Amendment.
perturbation.changed_text: The Partnership, the Partners and Manager desire to amend the Agreement to extend the term of the Agreement and to add a new section concerning curtailment of the LIFO Partners (as defined below), all as set forth in this Amendment. NOW, THEREFORE, IN CONSIDERATION of the foregoing premises, the parties hereby agree as follows: 1. Amendment. The parties acknowledge that this agreement may be terminated without cause, or recourse to legal action.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement does not explicitly state that termination can occur without cause or legal recourse. Under California law, there are implied covenants of good faith and fair dealing that may restrict the ability to terminate a contract without cause, and without any due process. Adding a clause that allows termination without cause and prohibits legal action contradicts these implied protections and could leave the Partners vulnerable. It is removing a legal avenue of consumer protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: California law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. This means that neither party can do anything that would deprive the other of the benefits of the agreement. Adding a clause that allows for termination without cause and barring legal action directly contradicts this covenant, as it allows one party to unilaterally end the agreement without any justification, potentially harming the other party's interests and eliminating their right to seek remedy for breach of contract.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CHAPTER 2. GENERAL DEFINITIONS AND PRINCIPLES OF INTERPRETATION - Sections 1201-1210 :: California Commercial Code :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Commercial Code Sections 1201-1210 CHAPTER 2. GENERAL DEFINITIONS AND PRINCIPLES OF INTERPRETATION COMMERCIAL CODE SECTION 1201-1210 1201. The following definitions apply for purposes of this code, subject to additional definitions contained in the subsequent divisions of this code that apply to specific divisions or chapters thereof, and unless the context otherwise requires: (1) "Action," in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceedings in which rights are determined. (2) "Aggrieved party" means a party entitled to resort to a remedy. (3) "Agreement" means the bargain of the parties in fact as found in their language or by implication from other circumstances, including course of dealing, usage of trade, and course of performance as provided in this code (Sections 1205, 2208, and 10207). Whether an agreement has legal consequences is determined by the provisions of this code, if applicable, and otherwise by the law of contracts (Section 1103). (Compare "contract.") (4) "Bank" means any person engaged in the business of banking. (5) "Bearer" means the person in possession of an instrument, document of title, or certificated security payable to bearer or endorsed in blank. (6) "Bill of lading" means a document evidencing the receipt of goods for shipment issued by a person engaged in the business of transporting or forwarding goods, and that, by its terms, evidences the intention of the issuer that the person entitled under the document (Section 7403(4)) has the right to receive, hold, and dispose of the document and the goods it covers. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause allowing termination without cause and barring legal action, which contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under California law, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. This covenant ensures neither party deprives the other of the agreement's benefits. The added clause allows one party to unilaterally end the agreement without justification, harming the other party's interests and preventing remedy for breach of contract. While the provided snippets do not explicitly mention the covenant of good faith, they define 'agreement' and 'aggrieved party,' which are foundational concepts. The alteration removes a potential legal protection, supporting a YES assessment.
